content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 24, 2019

David Nam
Deputy City Attorney II
City of Vacaville
650 Merchant St.
Vacaville, CA 95688

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-170

Dear Mr. Nam:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090.¹
Please note that we are only providing advice under Section 1090, not under other general conflict
of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code.
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the
case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional
advice.

We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts
relative to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Solano County District Attorney's
Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from
either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of
Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any
individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Vacaville from entering a contract for project
management services with a consultant where the consultant previously provided services under a
separate contract on the same project?

_______________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-170
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

No. As explained below, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering a project
management services contract with the consultant who previously provided services under a
separate contract on the same project.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are seeking advice on behalf of the City of Vacaville concerning the conflict of interest
provisions under Section 1090. The City seeks to hire consultant NexLevel, a division of SDI
Presence LLC ("SDI"), to perform project management services on a City project for which SDI
previously provided vendor procurement and selection services.²

First Contract

Under the initial contract, the City hired SDI to provide "Enterprise Resource Planning
(ERP) Procurement and Selection Consulting Services." An ERP system is the system used by the
City for all of its financials (including accounts receivable, accounts payable, general ledger,
purchasing, grants, projects, and fixed assets) as well as human resources (payroll, employee
records management, recruitment, timesheets, reporting) and utility billing. The system is used by
every department within the City. The project to migrate to a new system is complex and is broken
down into a number of phases over several years. In addition to installing and learning a new
system, the project also includes converting data from the previous system into the new one.

Given the complex nature of the project, SDI assisted in facilitating the ERP selection and
procurement process, making sure the City clearly communicated all ERP requirements in the RFP,
ensured the City followed a thorough vetting process for all proposals, and helped the City navigate
the highly technical contract process to select a technology services vendor. As a part of the
selection and procurement, SDI was required to, among other things: draft a work plan; hold
meetings with City staff; review relevant City documents; conduct workshops with various City
departments; draft a Request for Proposal (RFP) for a technology services vendor; review said RFP
with City staff, and identify/notify potential vendors; facilitate the bidding process, prepare
evaluation packets, screen proposal, prepare a vendor short-list; negotiate with vendors; and assist
with City Council approval of the proposed vendor.

As part of its proposal submitted to the City, SDI included "Project Management" as an
optional service, stating it would be "available and prepared to provide ongoing ERP
implementation and support. Its project management services would include "project management,
chart of accounts analysis and development, conversion assistance, quality assurance, testing
support, and other services that help ensure a successful implementation."

Although the City indicated some amount of project management support would be
necessary during the implementation phase, it was initially uncertain as to the level of support it
would need. Ultimately, at the suggestion of the City Attorney, the City decided to wait to do a

² You provided the first agreement titled "NexLevel – SDI Consulting Agreement" between the City and SDI
and the proposed second agreement titled "Enterprise Resource Planning Project Implementation."

File No. A-19-170
Page No. 3

separate future agreement for the project management in order to better determine what level of
services was needed.³

Second Contract

The City recently retained the services of a technology services company, Tyler
Technologies, as a result of the above process and now desires to enter another agreement with SDI
to provide project management services. The proposed contract requires that SDI assist in initiating
the project which may include, among other tasks, identification and setup of an implementation
team workspace and review of a Project Team Contact List. During the planning phase, SDI would
be required to assist in the development, review, and/or acceptance of all necessary project
management documents.

In addition, during the execution phase of the project, the proposed contract would require
SDI to assist with executing the relevant plans, schedules, procedures, and standards as prepared for
the project, including such tasks as business process review, data conversion, testing, training,
documentation development and quality assurance. Lastly, during the monitoring and control phase,
SDI would assist in reviewing the project schedule (including project progress pertaining to the
completion of tasks, deliverables, and milestones) and reviewing regular status reports, attendance
at project status meetings, and the tracking of issues, action items, and risks.

The proposed contract states that during the implementation, SDI would be working with
the City Project Manager and possibly with the Tyler Technologies' Project Manager, as directed
by the City, to assist in all phases of the implementation. It further states the City may determine
that some tasks in the Scope of Services will not need to be completed or can be completed by City
staff, and the specific services to be provided by SDI would be identified by the City's project
manager.

You provided further information by email on September 13, 2019, stating that SDI was not
in a position to, and did not influence or affect the level of the proposed project management
services through the services it provided under the initial contract. You also clarified that prior to
entering into a contract for implementation support, City staff wanted to fully understand all the
components and necessary resources required for the implementation phase in order to identify the
appropriate level of effort that would be required from SDI. This was done mainly through
discussions with the selected ERP vendor and speaking with other cities who had completed similar
implementations. Additionally, the City wanted to determine the workload of the City's project
manager and associated staff. All of this happened independent of SDI.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the

³ see City email from GeorgeAnne Meggers-Smith dated October 31, 2017.

File No. A-19-170
Page No. 4

making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38
Cal.3d 633, 646.)

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district,
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

Independent Contractors Subject to Section 1090

Courts have long found that independent contractors that serve in advisory positions that
have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency
are subject to Section 1090. (See Hub City v. City of Compton (2010) 186
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.2d 278, 291 ("statutes
prohibiting personal interests of public officers in public contracts are strictly enforced. [Citation.]
... [A] person merely in an advisory position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest rule"].)
This long-standing rule was recently affirmed by the California Supreme Court (People v. Superior
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230), and it applies equally to corporate consultants. (Davis v.
Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300.)

The purpose behind this inclusiveness of the definition is to ensure that independent
contractors who are essentially performing a public function, though temporarily, provide the same
"fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965).)
In Hub City, the court stated that a person's status as an official under Section 1090 "turns on the
extent to which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a
capacity that demands the public trust." (Hub City, supra, 186 Cal.App.4th at p. 1125.)

Most recently in Sahlolbei, the California Supreme Court addressed the standards for
applying Section 1090's prohibitions to contracts made by a public entity's independent contractors,
consultants and advisors. (Sahlolbei, supra, 3 Cal.5th at p. 234.) Prosecutors alleged that the CIO
of a public hospital asked one of its influential surgeons, Sahlolbei, to assist in recruiting physicians
for the hospital medical staff. (Ibid.) It was further alleged that Sahlolbei then recruited an
anesthesiologist and profited from the resulting employment contract through payments similar to
kick-backs. (Ibid.)

The Supreme Court concluded that Section 1090's reference to "officers" applies to "outside
advisors with responsibilities for public contracting similar to those belonging to formal officers"
(Id. at p. 237), and held that not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090 – liability
extends only to independent contractors who can be said to have been entrusted with "transact[ing]
on behalf of the Government" (Id. at p. 240 quoting Stigall, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 570.)

Here, the initial contract between the City and SDI required SDI to assist the City in the
selection of a technology services vendor by providing various procurement services. Based on the
facts provided, SDI is subject to Section 1090 because, as a consultant under the initial contract, it
had responsibilities to assist in the City's public contracting, including preparing an RFP for a
technology services vendor and assisting with City Council approval of the desired vendor.

File No. A-19-170
Page No. 5

Participating in Making a Contract

The next issue is whether SDI, as a consultant to the City under the initial contract and
subject to Section 1090, participated in the making of the proposed City contract for project
management services for the same project.

With respect to making a contract, Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who
participate personally in the actual execution of the contract to capture those officials who
participate in any way in the making of the contract. (People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046,
1052.) Therefore, participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and
specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae
(1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237.)

In addition, Section 1090 prohibits self-dealing. (See Hub City, supra, 186 Cal.App.4th at p.
1124 [independent contractor leveraged his public position for access to city officials and
influenced them for his pecuniary benefit]; California Housing Finance Agency v. Hanover (2007)
148 Cal.App.4th 682, 690 ["Section 1090 places responsibility for acts of self-dealing on the public
servant where he or she exercises sufficient control over the public entity, i.e., where the agent is in
a position to contract in his or her official capacity"]; Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th
1050, 1090 [The purpose of Section 1090 is to prohibit self-dealing, not representation of the
interests of others].)

As mentioned, SDI was tasked with preparing an RFP for a technology services vendor and
assisting with City Council approval of the desired vendor under the initial contract. Importantly,
SDI did not submit a bid for and did not attempt to secure the contract to become the City's
technology services vendor – it only assisted the City in finding a company, Tyler Technologies, to
perform the services set forth in the RFP that it helped draft.

Now that the City has a technology services vendor, the proposed second contract with SDI
would require that it provide project management services such as assisting with identification and
setup of an implementation team workspace, assisting in the development, review, and/or
acceptance of all necessary project management documents, assisting with executing the relevant
plans, schedules, procedures, and standards as prepared for the project and assisting in the review of
the project schedule and status reports.

You stated that although the City contemplated having SDI perform project management
services as part of the first contract, the City ultimately decided to wait until after the technology
services vendor was chosen in order to better determine what level of project management services
would be necessary. According to your facts, SDI was not able to affect the level of project
management services required in the currently proposed contract through its performance of
services in the initial contract. The decision concerning the level of these services was ultimately
informed by discussions with Tyler Technologies, other cities who had completed similar
implementations and with City's own project manager and associated staff – all independent of
SDI.

File No. A-19-170
Page No. 6

Pursuant to the facts you provided and based on the authority cited above, we find that SDI
did not participate in the making of the currently proposed contract for project management services
through its initial contract with the City.

Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering the proposed contract
for project management services with SDI.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: [signature]
Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:aja

Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Vacaville from entering a contract for project 
management services with a consultant where the consultant previously provided services under a 
separate contract on the same project?

No. As explained below, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering a project 
management services contract with the consultant who previously provided services unde·r a 
separate contract on the same project.

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the 
3 See City email from George Anne Meggers-Smith dated October 31, 2017.  
making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 . 
Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
Independent Contractors Subject to Section 1090 
Courts have long found that independent contractors that serve in advisory positions that 
have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency 
are subject to Section 1090. (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 291 ["statutes 
prohibiting personal interests of public officers in public contracts are strictly enforced. [Citation.] 
... [,] A person merely in an advisory position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest rule"].) 
This long-standing rule was recently affirmed by the California Supreme Court (People v. Superior 
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230), and it applies equally to corporate consultants. (Davis v. 
Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300.) 
The purpose behind this inclusiveness of the definition is to ensure that independent 
contractors who are essentially performing a public function, though temporarily, provide the same 
"fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965).) 
In Hub City, the court stated that a person's status as an official under Section 1090 "turns on the 
extent to which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a 
capacity that demands the public trust." (Hub City, supra, 186 Cal.App.4th at p. 1125.) 
Most recently in Sahlolbei, the California Supreme Court addressed the standards for 
applying Section 1090's prohibitions to contracts made by a public entity's independent contractors, 
consultants and advisors. (Sahlolbei, supr(l, 3 Cal.5th at p. 234.) Prosecutors alleged that the CEO 
of a public hospital asked one of its influential surgeons, Sahlolbei, to assist in recruiting physicians 
for the hospital medical staff. (Ibid.) It was further alleged that Sahlolbei then recruited an 
anesthesiologist and profited from the resulting employment contract through payments similar to 
kick-backs. (Ibid.) 
The Supreme Court concluded that Section 1090's reference to "officers" applies to "outside 
advisors with responsibilities for public contracting similar to those belonging to formal officers" 
(Id. at p. 237), and held that not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090- liability 
extends only to independent contractors who can be said to have been entrusted with "transact[ing] 
on behalf of the Government" (Id. at p. 240 quoting Stigall, supra, 58 Cal.2d at p. 570.) 
Here, the initial contract between the City and SDI required SDI to assist the City in the 
selection of a technology services vendor by providing various procurement services. Based on the 
facts provided, SDI is subject to Section 1090 because, as a consultant under the, initial contract, it 
had responsibilities to assist in the City's public contracting, including preparing an RFP for a 
technology services vendor and assisting with City Council approval of the desired vendor. 


Participating in Making a Contract 
FileNo. A-19-170  
The next issue is whether SDI, as a consultant to the City under the initial contract and 
subject to Section 1090, participated in the making of the proposed City contract for project 
management services for the same project. 
With respect to making a contract, Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who 
participate personally in the actual execution of the contract to capture those officials who 
participate in any way in the making of the contract. (People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 
1052.) Therefore, participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving 
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and 
specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae 
(1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237.) 
In addition, Section 1090 prohibits self-dealing. (See Hub City, supra, 186 Cal.App.4th at p. 
1124 [independent contractor leveraged his public position for access to city officials and 
influenced them for his pecuniary benefit]; California Ho_using Finance Agency v. Hanover (2007) 
148 Cal.App.4th 682, 690 ["Section 1090 places responsibility for acts of self-dealing on the public 
servant where he or she exercises sufficient control over the public entity, i.e., where the agent is in 
a position to contract in his or her official capacity"]; Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 
1050, 1090 [The purpose of Section 1090 is to prohibit self-dealing, not representation of the 
interests of others].) 
As mentioned, SDI was tasked with preparing an RFP for a technology services vendor and 
assisting with City Council approval of the desired vendor under the initial contract. Importantly, 
SDI did not submit a bid for and did not attempt to secure the contract to become the City's 
technology services vendor - it only assisted the City in finding a company, Tyler Technologies, to 
perform the services set forth in the RFP that it helped draft. 
Now that the City has a technology services vendor, the proposed second contract with SDI 
would require that it provide project management services such as assisting with identification and 
setup of an implementation team workspace, assisting in the development, review, and/or 
acceptance of all necessary project management documents, assisting with executing the relevant 
plans, schedules, procedures, and standards as prepared for the project and assisting in the review of 
the project schedule and status reports. 
You stated that although the City contemplated having SDI perform project management 
services as part of the first contract, the City ultimately decided to wait until after the technology 
services vendor was chosen in order to better determine what level of project management services 
would be necessary. According to your facts, SDI Was not able to affect the level of project 
management services required in the currently proposed contract through its performance of 
services in the initial contract. The decision concerning the level of these services was ultimately 
informed by discussions with Tyler Technologies, other cities who had completed similar 
implementations and with City's own project manager and associated staff- all independent of 
SDI. 


FileNo. A-19-170  
Pursuant to the facts you provided and based on the authority cited above, we find that SDI 
did not participate in the making of the currently proposed contract for project management services 
through its initial contract with the City. 
· 
Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering the proposed contract 
for project management services with SDI.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Vacaville from entering a contract for project 
management services with a consultant where the consultant previously provided services under a 
separate contract on the same project?

CONCLUSION: No. As explained below, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering a project 
management services contract with the consultant who previously provided services unde·r a 
separate contract on the same project.
